name: Zambia
tagline: From Iron Age farmers and the kingdoms of the Zambezi and Luapula to the Copperbelt, Northern Rhodesia, and a landlocked African republic.
summary: Zambia occupies a high plateau in the heart of south-central Africa, drained by the Zambezi and the Luapula. Bantu-speaking farmers and ironworkers settled the region over the first millennium CE, displacing or absorbing earlier Khoisan and Batwa hunter-gatherers; among the peoples who emerged were the Tonga of the southern plateau, and later the Lozi of the upper Zambezi floodplain, the Bemba of the north-east, the Chewa of the east, and the Lunda kingdom of Kazembe on the Luapula. From the eighteenth century these states were drawn into long-distance trade in copper, ivory, and captives, and in the nineteenth century the region was disrupted by Swahili-Arab and Chikunda slave raiders and by Ngoni warbands fleeing the upheavals of southern Africa. The Scottish missionary David Livingstone reached the great falls the local peoples called Mosi-oa-Tunya in 1855. From the 1890s Cecil Rhodes's British South Africa Company extended its rule over the territory, which became the protectorate of Northern Rhodesia, its economy soon dominated by the vast copper deposits of the Copperbelt. African opposition to the white-settler Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland (1953–1963) fed a nationalist movement led by Kenneth Kaunda and the United National Independence Party, and on 24 October 1964 Zambia became an independent republic with Kaunda as its first president. After decades of one-party rule under his philosophy of Humanism and support for southern African liberation movements, Zambia returned to multiparty politics in 1991; it remains a copper-dependent state that has wrestled with debt and seen repeated peaceful transfers of power.

founding.label: Independence from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: Zambia became a sovereign republic on 24 October 1964, when the British protectorate of Northern Rhodesia gained independence and Kenneth Kaunda took office as its first president. The date capped a long struggle against the white-settler-dominated Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland and the colonial order that the British South Africa Company had imposed from the 1890s. The new nation took its name from the Zambezi River and inherited an economy built around the copper mines of the Copperbelt. Beneath that colonial frame lay a far older history of Iron Age farmers, the Lozi, Bemba, Tonga, Chewa, and the Lunda kingdom of Kazembe, and centuries of trade and upheaval across the south-central African plateau.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: From the United Kingdom on 24 October 1964; a republic from the outset

quickFacts.label: First president
quickFacts.value: Kenneth Kaunda, leader of the United National Independence Party (UNIP)

quickFacts.label: Name origin
quickFacts.value: Named after the Zambezi River, which forms much of its southern border

quickFacts.label: Colonial era
quickFacts.value: Ruled by the British South Africa Company, then as the protectorate of Northern Rhodesia from 1924

quickFacts.label: Economy
quickFacts.value: Long dependent on copper from the Copperbelt; defaulted on its sovereign debt in 2020

quickFacts.label: Government
quickFacts.value: Multiparty republic since 1991, with repeated peaceful transfers of power

eras.title: Iron Age farmers and the Bantu migrations
eras.standfirst: Bantu-speaking farmers and ironworkers spread across the plateau over the first millennium CE, settling the land of earlier hunter-gatherers and giving rise to peoples such as the Tonga of the southern Zambezi.
body: Long before farming reached the region, the high plateau of what is now Zambia was home to Khoisan and Batwa hunter-gatherers, whose presence survives in rock art at sites such as the Mwela paintings near Kasama. From around the third century CE, Bantu-speaking peoples migrating from the north and west began to settle the area, bringing iron-smelting technology, cattle, and cultivated crops. Over the following centuries these incoming farmers gradually displaced, absorbed, or intermarried with the earlier populations, transforming the landscape into one of villages, fields, and ironworking.

Among the peoples who took shape on the southern plateau were the Tonga (Ba-Tonga) and the related Ila, settled along the middle Zambezi and its tributaries. Lacking centralised kingdoms, the Tonga lived in dispersed agricultural communities organised around lineage and locality, a pattern that would distinguish them from the later monarchies of the Lozi and Bemba. Their long occupation of the Zambezi valley is documented through oral tradition, comparative linguistics, and archaeology.

The region's place in wider trade networks is shown by the Iron Age site of Ingombe Ilede, near the confluence of the Zambezi and Kafue. Excavations there revealed burials with copper crosses, gold, glass beads, and imported cloth, evidence that by roughly the fifteenth century the inhabitants were linked, through intermediaries, to the gold and ivory trade of the Zimbabwe plateau and the Indian Ocean coast. Such finds testify to a south-central Africa already connected to distant commerce centuries before any European reached the interior.

events.title: Bantu farmers settle the plateau
events.summary: Bantu-speaking migrants bringing iron-working, cattle, and agriculture spread across the region over the first millennium CE, gradually settling land held by Khoisan and Batwa hunter-gatherers.

events.title: The Tonga of the Zambezi
events.summary: On the southern plateau the Tonga and related Ila peoples established dispersed farming communities along the middle Zambezi, organised by lineage rather than centralised kingship.

events.title: Trade at Ingombe Ilede
events.summary: The Iron Age settlement of Ingombe Ilede near the Zambezi yielded copper crosses, gold, and imported beads and cloth, showing the region's links to the Indian Ocean trade in gold and ivory.

pullquote.text: By the fifteenth century the people of Ingombe Ilede were already trading copper, gold, and ivory along networks reaching the Indian Ocean coast.

eras.title: Kingdoms of the Zambezi and Luapula: Lozi, Bemba, Chewa and Kazembe
eras.standfirst: Centralised states emerged across the region — the Lozi kingdom of the upper Zambezi floodplain, the Bemba of the north-east, the Chewa of the east, and the Lunda kingdom of Kazembe on the Luapula, which grew rich on copper, ivory, and trade.
body: On the seasonally flooded plain of the upper Zambezi, the Lozi (or Barotse) kingdom took shape from the seventeenth century. Its people, originally called the Luyana or Aluyi and tracing their origins to the Lunda world to the north, were ruled by a king titled the Litunga, whose court moved between flood-season and dry-season capitals as the waters rose and fell. The kingdom's centralised administration over the Bulozi floodplain made it one of the most distinctive states in the region.

To the north-east, the Bemba established a kingdom under a paramount chief, the Chitimukulu, expanding across the high plateau between the great lakes; like the Lozi and Kazembe, Bemba traditions trace their roots back to the Lunda and Luba states of the Congo basin. In the east, the Chewa formed part of the wider Maravi cultural world that spanned the borderlands of present-day Zambia, Malawi, and Mozambique. The most commercially powerful state, however, lay on the Luapula in the north: the Lunda kingdom of Kazembe.

Founded in the early eighteenth century when an offshoot of the Lunda crossed the Luapula River and subdued the local Shila people, the kingdom of the Mwata Kazembe controlled the fisheries of Lake Mweru and the trade in copper from nearby Katanga, salt, and ivory. It became a hub of long-distance commerce linking the Atlantic and Indian Ocean trading systems; Portuguese expeditions led by Francisco de Lacerda in 1798 and António Gamitto in 1831–1832 reached the Mwata Kazembe's capital seeking trade and treaties. By the early nineteenth century the Bemba and Kazembe's Lunda were increasingly drawn into selling ivory and captives to traders from the east coast.

events.title: Rise of the Lozi kingdom
events.summary: On the upper Zambezi floodplain the Luyana (Aluyi) people, of Lunda origin, built the Lozi (Barotse) kingdom ruled by a king titled the Litunga, with capitals that shifted with the annual flood.

events.title: Founding of the Kazembe kingdom
events.summary: An offshoot of the Lunda crossed the Luapula River, subdued the Shila, and established the kingdom of the Mwata Kazembe, which grew wealthy on Lake Mweru fisheries and trade in copper, salt, and ivory.

events.title: Lacerda reaches Kazembe
events.summary: A Portuguese expedition under Francisco de Lacerda reached the court of the Mwata Kazembe seeking to open trade links across the continent, an early European contact with the Luapula kingdom.

events.title: Gamitto's mission to Kazembe
events.summary: A second Portuguese expedition, led by António Gamitto, visited the Mwata Kazembe, leaving a detailed account of a Lunda state deeply engaged in the regional trade in ivory, copper, and captives.

figures.name: Mwata Kazembe
figures.role: Title of the Lunda kings of the Luapula
figures.blurb: Dynastic title of the rulers of the Kazembe kingdom on the Luapula, who commanded the region's fisheries and its trade in copper, salt, and ivory and received Portuguese envoys.

pullquote.text: On the Luapula the Mwata Kazembe ruled a kingdom that traded copper, salt, and ivory between the Atlantic and Indian Ocean worlds.

eras.title: Upheaval, the slave trade and Livingstone
eras.standfirst: The nineteenth century brought violent disruption — Ngoni warbands from the south, the Kololo conquest of the Lozi, and the spread of the Swahili-Arab and Chikunda trade in ivory and captives — even as David Livingstone opened the region to European eyes.
body: The upheavals of the early nineteenth century, set off by the rise of the Zulu state and the wars known as the Mfecane in southern Africa, sent armed migrant peoples surging northward. Around 1840 the Kololo, a Sotho group fleeing these conflicts under their leader Sebitwane, crossed the Zambezi and conquered the weakened Lozi kingdom, imposing their own rule and language on the floodplain. Further east, Ngoni warbands under leaders such as Zwangendaba crossed the Zambezi in the 1830s, raiding and absorbing peoples as they moved and settling in the eastern parts of the region.

At the same time the region was drawn ever deeper into a brutal long-distance commerce. Swahili-Arab traders pushing inland from the Indian Ocean coast, together with Chikunda raiders from the Zambezi valley and African intermediaries such as the Bisa and Yao, exchanged guns and cloth for ivory and enslaved people, who were marched to the coast for sale. This trade brought firearms, disruption, and depopulation to many districts, and intertwined with the political turmoil of the era to reshape the human map of the plateau. The traffic in human beings inflicted suffering on an immense scale and would later be invoked to justify European intervention.

Into this world came the Scottish missionary and explorer David Livingstone. On 16 November 1855, guided by local people during his transcontinental journey, he became the first European recorded to see the great waterfall on the Zambezi that the inhabitants called Mosi-oa-Tunya, 'the smoke that thunders'; he renamed it Victoria Falls after the British queen. Livingstone's accounts of the region and his denunciations of the slave trade drew European attention to south-central Africa and helped open the way for the missionaries, traders, and empire-builders who followed. Meanwhile the Lozi threw off Kololo rule in 1864, restoring their kingdom under Sipopa.

events.title: Kololo conquest of the Lozi
events.summary: The Kololo, a Sotho people fleeing the wars of the Mfecane under Sebitwane, crossed the Zambezi and conquered the Lozi kingdom, imposing their rule and language on the floodplain.

events.title: Ngoni incursions from the south
events.summary: Ngoni warbands driven north by the upheavals of the Mfecane crossed the Zambezi under leaders such as Zwangendaba, raiding and absorbing peoples and settling in the east of the region.

events.title: Livingstone reaches Victoria Falls
events.summary: Guided by local people, David Livingstone became the first European recorded to see the falls the inhabitants called Mosi-oa-Tunya, renaming them Victoria Falls and drawing European attention to the region.

events.title: Swahili-Arab and Chikunda slave trade
events.summary: Swahili-Arab traders from the coast and Chikunda raiders from the Zambezi, with Bisa and Yao intermediaries, exchanged guns and cloth for ivory and captives, bringing violence and depopulation to the plateau.

events.title: Lozi restoration under Sipopa
events.summary: A Lozi rebellion overthrew Kololo rule and restored the kingdom under Sipopa, reviving the indigenous monarchy of the upper Zambezi floodplain.

figures.name: David Livingstone
figures.blurb: Scottish missionary and explorer who in 1855 became the first European recorded to reach Mosi-oa-Tunya, which he renamed Victoria Falls; his writings publicised the region and condemned the slave trade.

pullquote.text: The inhabitants knew the falls as Mosi-oa-Tunya, 'the smoke that thunders', long before Livingstone renamed them for his queen in 1855.

eras.title: Company rule, Northern Rhodesia and the Copperbelt
eras.standfirst: Cecil Rhodes's British South Africa Company extended its rule through treaties and concessions, creating Northern Rhodesia, while the discovery of vast copper deposits in the 1920s built the mining economy that would define the country.
body: European empire reached the plateau through Cecil Rhodes's chartered British South Africa Company. In 1890 the company secured the Lochner Concession from the Lozi king Lewanika, who granted mineral rights in exchange for promises of protection, and through this and other treaties the company asserted control over the territories north of the Zambezi. Administered under royal charter, the lands were organised as North-Western and North-Eastern Rhodesia and amalgamated in 1911 into a single territory named Northern Rhodesia. In 1924 the British Crown took over administration directly, making Northern Rhodesia a protectorate under the Colonial Office.

The territory's destiny was transformed by copper. Rich sulphide ores were proven along the Copperbelt of the north in the late 1920s, and from the 1930s large mines drew tens of thousands of African workers and a smaller community of white miners and managers into a new industrial economy. The colonial state's revenues, towns, and railways came to revolve around copper exports; the capital was moved from Livingstone to the more central Lusaka in 1935. But the mines were also crucibles of grievance: African workers, paid far less than whites and bound by a discriminatory colour bar, struck in 1935 — when six men were shot dead during disturbances on the Copperbelt — and again in 1940.

Colonial Northern Rhodesia was a society sharply divided by race. A small settler population dominated the Legislative Council, while the African majority had little political voice. The labour migration drawn by the mines, the growth of towns, and the experience of industrial work nonetheless created new networks and grievances among Africans that, combined with the example of trade unionism, would feed the nationalist politics of the following decades.

events.title: The Lochner Concession
events.summary: The Lozi king Lewanika granted mineral rights to Cecil Rhodes's British South Africa Company in exchange for promises of protection, opening the territory to company rule.

events.title: Creation of Northern Rhodesia
events.summary: The British South Africa Company amalgamated North-Western and North-Eastern Rhodesia into a single territory named Northern Rhodesia, administered under royal charter.

events.title: Northern Rhodesia becomes a Crown protectorate
events.summary: Administration passed from the British South Africa Company to the Colonial Office, making Northern Rhodesia a British protectorate; the capital later moved to Lusaka in 1935.

events.title: Copperbelt copper proven
events.summary: Rich copper sulphide ores were proven beneath the Copperbelt, and large-scale mining from the 1930s built the industrial economy that came to dominate the territory.

events.title: Copperbelt mineworkers' strike
events.summary: African mineworkers struck over taxes and conditions; six men were shot dead during the disturbances, an early sign of the labour grievances that would fuel nationalist politics. Further strikes followed in 1940.

figures.name: Lewanika
figures.blurb: Lozi king who consolidated the Barotse kingdom and in 1890 signed the Lochner Concession with the British South Africa Company, seeking protection but opening his country to colonial rule.

figures.name: Cecil Rhodes
figures.role: Imperialist and founder of the British South Africa Company
figures.blurb: British imperialist whose chartered British South Africa Company secured concessions north of the Zambezi, bringing the lands that became Northern Rhodesia under company rule.

pullquote.text: Copper made colonial Northern Rhodesia: its towns, railways, and revenues all came to revolve around the mines of the Copperbelt.

eras.title: Federation, nationalism and independence
eras.standfirst: African opposition to the white-settler Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland fuelled a nationalist movement led by Kenneth Kaunda and UNIP, culminating in independence as the Republic of Zambia on 24 October 1964.
body: In 1953 Britain joined Northern Rhodesia, Southern Rhodesia, and Nyasaland into the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland, a union dominated by the white settlers of Southern Rhodesia. Most Africans bitterly opposed it, fearing it would entrench settler power and the colour bar at their expense, and the Federation became the catalyst for organised nationalism. The African National Congress, led by Harry Nkumbula, campaigned against it, but frustration with Nkumbula's caution drove younger militants to break away.

In October 1958 Kenneth Kaunda, a teacher and former ANC secretary-general from Chinsali, founded the more radical Zambia African National Congress; it was banned in March 1959 and Kaunda imprisoned. On his release he took the leadership of its successor, the United National Independence Party (UNIP), which mounted a campaign of mass civil disobedience known as the Cha-cha-cha to make the territory ungovernable and force the pace of change. Mounting African resistance, the parallel crisis in Nyasaland, and shifting British policy made the Federation untenable, and it was dissolved at the end of December 1963.

Elections under a new constitution gave UNIP a decisive majority, and Kaunda became prime minister in January 1964. On 24 October 1964 Northern Rhodesia became the independent Republic of Zambia, taking its name from the Zambezi, with Kenneth Kaunda as its first president. The new state inherited a copper economy of great wealth but also deep inequalities, a small educated elite, and the unfinished business of a southern Africa still ruled by white-minority regimes on its borders.

events.title: Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland
events.summary: Britain joined Northern Rhodesia, Southern Rhodesia, and Nyasaland into a federation dominated by white settlers, provoking widespread African opposition and galvanising nationalist politics.

events.title: Kaunda founds ZANC
events.summary: Breaking with Harry Nkumbula's ANC, Kenneth Kaunda founded the radical Zambia African National Congress; it was banned in 1959 and Kaunda imprisoned, but the movement continued as UNIP.

events.title: The Cha-cha-cha campaign
events.summary: Under Kaunda, UNIP launched a campaign of mass civil disobedience known as the Cha-cha-cha against colonial rule and the Federation, intensifying pressure for African self-government.

events.title: Federation dissolved
events.summary: Sustained African opposition and shifting British policy brought the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland to an end, opening the way to independence for its territories.

events.title: Independence of Zambia
events.summary: Northern Rhodesia became the independent Republic of Zambia, named after the Zambezi, with Kenneth Kaunda as its first president.

figures.name: Kenneth Kaunda
figures.blurb: Teacher and nationalist leader who founded ZANC, led UNIP, and brought Zambia to independence in 1964, governing for 27 years under his philosophy of Humanism before accepting multiparty elections in 1991.

figures.name: Harry Nkumbula
figures.role: Nationalist leader, president of the ANC
figures.blurb: Veteran nationalist who led the African National Congress against the Federation; his caution prompted younger militants under Kaunda to break away and form a more radical movement.

pullquote.text: On 24 October 1964 Northern Rhodesia became Zambia, taking its name from the Zambezi, with Kenneth Kaunda as first president.

eras.title: The republic: Humanism, multiparty politics and modern Zambia
eras.standfirst: Independent Zambia passed through one-party rule under Kaunda's Humanism and support for liberation movements, a peaceful return to multiparty democracy in 1991, and a modern era marked by copper dependence, debt, and repeated transfers of power.
body: Kenneth Kaunda governed Zambia for 27 years, espousing a national philosophy he called Humanism — a blend of African communal values and socialism intended to guide development. In 1972 he made Zambia a one-party state, formalised in the 1973 constitution, with UNIP the sole legal party and Kaunda re-elected unopposed at intervals thereafter. Internationally he made Zambia a frontline state, sheltering and supporting southern African liberation movements — including South Africa's ANC, Zimbabwe's ZANU and ZAPU, and Namibia's SWAPO — a stance that brought retaliatory raids and heavy economic costs as the country's borders with white-ruled neighbours were repeatedly closed or contested.

Zambia's fortunes were chained to copper. In the early 1970s copper provided the overwhelming majority of export earnings, but the world price collapsed from the mid-1970s, and with it the economy. Years of falling revenues, rising debt, mismanagement, and austerity brought hardship; food-price riots in 1990 and a failed coup attempt signalled the depth of discontent. Under mounting pressure Kaunda lifted the ban on opposition parties, and in the multiparty elections of 1991 — judged broadly free and fair — Frederick Chiluba and the Movement for Multiparty Democracy swept to power. Kaunda accepted defeat and handed over peacefully, a rare and consequential transfer of power in the Africa of its day.

Chiluba's governments pursued economic liberalisation and the privatisation of the copper mines, but corruption allegations dogged his rule, and his bid for a third term in 2001 was blocked by public and parliamentary resistance. He was succeeded by Levy Mwanawasa, who died in office in 2008, then by Rupiah Banda, Michael Sata (who died in office in 2014), and Edgar Lungu. The economy remained heavily dependent on copper and increasingly burdened by debt; in November 2020 Zambia became the first African country of the COVID-19 era to default on its sovereign Eurobond debt. In August 2021 the opposition leader Hakainde Hichilema won the presidency in another peaceful, decisive transfer of power, taking office with a mandate to restructure the debt and revive a still copper-reliant economy.

events.title: One-party state and Humanism
events.summary: Kaunda made Zambia a one-party state under UNIP, formalised in the 1973 constitution, governing under his philosophy of Humanism, a blend of African communal values and socialism.

events.title: Copper collapse and economic decline
events.summary: Copper, which provided the bulk of export earnings, fell sharply on the world market from the mid-1970s, triggering a long economic decline, rising debt, and austerity.

events.title: Frontline state for liberation
events.summary: Zambia hosted and supported southern African liberation movements, including the ANC, ZANU, ZAPU, and SWAPO, becoming a frontline state that bore retaliatory raids and economic costs.

events.title: Return to multiparty democracy
events.summary: After food riots and mounting pressure, Kaunda lifted the ban on opposition parties; Frederick Chiluba and the Movement for Multiparty Democracy won the elections, and Kaunda handed over power peacefully.

events.title: Sovereign debt default
events.summary: Burdened by debt and falling revenues, Zambia became the first African country of the COVID-19 era to default on its Eurobonds after missing a coupon payment.

events.title: Hichilema elected president
events.summary: Opposition leader Hakainde Hichilema won the presidency in a decisive, peaceful election, taking office with a mandate to restructure the national debt and revive the copper-dependent economy.

figures.name: Frederick Chiluba
figures.blurb: Trade unionist who led the Movement for Multiparty Democracy to victory in 1991, ending Kaunda's long rule; his presidency liberalised the economy but was later marked by corruption allegations.

pullquote.text: Zambia's fortunes were chained to copper — and when the price collapsed from the mid-1970s, the economy fell with it.

figures.name: Kenneth Kaunda
figures.blurb: Led the United National Independence Party to independence in 1964 and governed for 27 years under his philosophy of Humanism, making Zambia a frontline state for southern African liberation before accepting multiparty elections in 1991.

figures.name: David Livingstone
figures.blurb: Scottish missionary who in 1855 became the first European recorded to reach Mosi-oa-Tunya, renaming it Victoria Falls and publicising south-central Africa and the horrors of the slave trade.

sources.label: Zambia: External contacts and the slave and ivory trade
Formation of the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland
The Road to Zambia's 2020 Sovereign Debt Default
